system_prompt:
Summarize this GitHub issue or pull request. The dedupe_summary field will be used for vector-based duplicate detection.

BAD dedupe_summary examples (too generic, would match unrelated issues):
- "Bug fix for an issue with the application" ← too vague
- "Updates configuration handling and fixes a reported problem" ← no specifics
- "Resolves issue where feature doesn't work as expected" ← says nothing

GOOD dedupe_summary examples (specific, would cluster correctly):
- "Discord REST API requests bypass proxy configuration, causing fetch failures in proxy-required environments"
- "WhatsApp group-level systemPrompt configuration not applied to message delivery"
- "Telegram editMessageCaption fails for non-photo media types due to missing content-type detection"

Remove from your analysis: template headers, checklists, testing instructions, environment details, reproduction steps, deployment notes. These add noise to embeddings.

Return JSON with keys: problem_summary, solution_summary, maintainer_signal_summary, dedupe_summary.
Plain text, no markdown, 1-3 sentences each. dedupe_summary is the most critical field.

results.title: Title channels.discord.proxy does not proxy REST API requests — guild resolve fails, guild channels broken behind HTTP proxy

summary.problem_summary: channels.discord.proxy only applies to the Discord WebSocket Gateway, not REST API startup requests (deploy commands, fetch-bot-identity) nor the guild resolve path. As a result, in environments that require an HTTP proxy the startup REST calls timeout with "fetch failed", guild:<id> remains unresolved, and guild channels are non-functional while DMs (over WebSocket) still work.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the configured channels.discord.proxy is applied to the undici dispatcher used for all Discord REST API calls during provider startup and the guild-resolve path (not just WebSocket). Consider adding a global proxy option that sets a ProxyAgent (setGlobalDispatcher) and add retry logic for guild resolution.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression reported on OpenClaw 2026.3.13 with reproduction steps, logs showing REST timeouts and guild unresolved, and verification that curl with the proxy works. User demonstrated a workaround by bootstrapping undici's ProxyAgent (NODE_OPTIONS) which fixes REST but not guild resolve, and suggests code change to route all Discord HTTP traffic through the proxy. Labels: bug, regression.
summary.dedupe_summary: channels.discord.proxy only proxies the Gateway websocket; Node v25/undici REST requests (deploy-rest, fetch-bot-identity) and the guild-resolve code path bypass the proxy causing "fetch failed" timeouts and guild channels to be unusable behind an HTTP proxy; global ProxyAgent workaround fixes REST but guild resolve still ignores the proxy.

judge.reasoning: Summarizes the core bug (proxy only applied to Gateway, REST and guild-resolve bypass proxy causing failures) and notes workaround, but includes a Node/undici version detail that is unnecessary for deduping.

results.title: [Bug]:channels.discord.proxy doesn’t apply to outbound REST sends / Carbon RequestClient fetch has no proxy support

summary.problem_summary: With channels.discord.proxy configured, Discord gateway (WebSocket) connects but REST API sends fail with "fetch failed" in proxy-required environments (e.g., China). Logs indicate REST proxy enabled but the RequestClient/Carbon fetch implementation doesn't actually use the proxy, causing outbound REST/attachment uploads to fail.
summary.solution_summary: Make the RequestClient/Carbon fetch honor channels.discord.proxy by adding proxy support (HTTP/SOCKS agents or a proxy-aware fetch implementation), ensure attachment downloads/uploads and all REST calls route through the configured proxy, and add tests and configuration parsing so the proxy is consistently applied.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Please prioritize a fix to have the Discord REST client use the configured channels.discord.proxy (including attachments) or provide an official workaround; backport to the 2026.2.x line if possible and include tests to prevent regressions for proxy-required regions.
summary.dedupe_summary: channels.discord.proxy not applied to outbound REST sends because Carbon RequestClient's fetch has no proxy support, causing fetch failures in proxy-required environments while WebSocket gateway still works.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that identifies the core bug (channels.discord.proxy not applied to REST) and root cause (Carbon RequestClient fetch lacks proxy support), without boilerplate or noisy details.

results.title: Discord can log in behind proxy, but native command deploy / bot identity fetch fail with 'fetch failed'

summary.problem_summary: When OpenClaw is configured with an HTTP proxy for Discord, the provider logs in and the gateway appears healthy but some startup REST calls consistently fail with "fetch failed", specifically native command deployment (/applications/{appId}/commands) and bot identity fetch, leaving the provider half-functional and triggering stale-socket health restarts.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure all Discord REST requests use the same proxy-aware fetch/dispatcher as the gateway/WebSocket path (unify transport code paths), and add retry/error handling for startup REST calls; user offered to provide environment details and test a patch.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show rest proxy and gateway proxy both enabled, applicationId fetch succeeds but deploy-rest:put and fetch-bot-identity fail with "fetch failed" across repeated restarts; issue reproducible on OpenClaw 2026.3.12 (config touched by 2026.3.13) and reporter can share environment or test fixes.
summary.dedupe_summary: OpenClaw Discord provider behind HTTP proxy (channels.discord.proxy=http://127.0.0.1:8119) logs into gateway but startup REST calls—deploying native commands to /applications/{id}/commands and fetching bot identity—fail with "fetch failed", indicating inconsistent proxy handling between REST and gateway paths.

judge.reasoning: Summary concisely describes the core issue—Discord provider logs in via gateway behind an HTTP proxy while REST startup calls to deploy commands and fetch bot identity fail with "fetch failed", indicating inconsistent proxy handling—using specific config and endpoints useful for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord proxy config recognized but REST API fails with fetch failed

summary.problem_summary: Discord channel proxy is logged as enabled but Discord REST API calls fail with 'fetch failed' when OpenClaw runs under WSL2 with an HTTP proxy; the gateway WebSocket never establishes (gatewayConnected=false).
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the configured channels.discord.proxy is passed to the underlying HTTP client used by discord.js (undici/fetch) and to the WebSocket transport so REST requests and gateway connections use an HTTP proxy agent; update OpenClaw to create and supply a proxy agent or set the appropriate environment/undici agent on startup.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reproducible 100% under WSL2 on OpenClaw 2026.3.13; logs show 'discord: rest proxy enabled' and 'discord: gateway proxy enabled' but REST calls return 'fetch failed' while curl via the same proxy succeeds. Severity: high (Discord channel unusable); config is ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json → channels.discord.proxy; workaround is TUN-mode transparent proxy or alternate channels.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord proxy configured (channels.discord.proxy) and logged as enabled, but discord.js REST/undici fetch calls fail with 'fetch failed' and the gateway WebSocket never connects under WSL2, despite curl -x to discord.com working; proxy not being applied to the underlying HTTP/fetch/WebSocket client.

judge.reasoning: The summary succinctly captures the core bug — the configured Discord proxy isn't being applied to the underlying undici/fetch/WebSocket clients causing REST 'fetch failed' errors and gateway connection failure — with specific tech terms and no template noise.

results.title: Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw's Discord REST API calls for sending messages ignore the configured proxy and fail with "TypeError: fetch failed" while the Discord WebSocket and curl requests through the same proxy work fine, indicating the REST path isn't proxy-aware.
summary.solution_summary: Route Discord REST API calls through the same proxy-aware fetch/SSRF guard path introduced for web_search/web_fetch so they honor channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY environment variables.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show "discord final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed"; repro with channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY set; Telegram and Discord WebSocket use the proxy successfully and curl POST to the Discord API via the proxy succeeds; changelog notes web tools fixed proxy routing but excluded Discord REST; suggested fix is to reuse the shared proxy-aware fetch path; webhook workaround exists but doesn't support replies.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API (message send) in OpenClaw bypasses proxy-aware fetch path and fails with "TypeError: fetch failed" despite channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY being set; WebSocket and curl via the proxy succeed, and the 2026.2.26 proxy fix for web_search/web_fetch did not include Discord REST.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that pinpoints Discord REST message sends bypassing the proxy-aware fetch path and failing with a TypeError despite proxy settings, so it will correctly cluster with similar proxy-related Discord REST issues.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to' routes to agent:*:main instead of live channel session

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the CLI command `openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...` resolves and delivers to the global agent:*:main session instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the CLI session resolver does not look up sessions by real delivery context and Discord targets are not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch to compiled dist files was applied: session resolution now searches by delivery context (channel + to + accountId), Discord target IDs are normalized (e.g. '148...' → 'channel:148...'), the compact resolver now uses buildAgentMainSessionKey and looks up delivery context, and the agent register command path now passes channel and replyAccount/accountId.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: HIGH risk: changes were made directly in compiled files under dist and will be lost on update; maintainers should port fixes to source, add tests for delivery-context session resolution and Discord target normalization, and add post-update validation (or a persistent CLI --session-key option) to prevent regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: CLI `openclaw agent --channel discord --to` routes to agent:*:main because the session resolver doesn't search by delivery context (channel+to+accountId), the command path didn't propagate channel/replyAccount, and Discord targets weren't normalized (e.g. '148...' vs 'channel:148...'); manual fixes applied to dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js, dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js, and dist/register.agent-DHoJWl4M.js to add delivery-context lookup and normalization.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that states the exact CLI command misrouting, the root cause (missing delivery-context lookup, command-path propagation, and target normalization), and the files changed, which is sufficient to cluster with similar session-resolver/delivery-context bugs.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads - volatile metadata re-injection + CLI routing to wrong session

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions: Discord thread messages were re-injecting volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) every turn, causing progressive context bloat; and CLI agent commands (openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...) resolved to the default main session (agent:*:main) instead of the live thread-bound session.
summary.solution_summary: Patched compiled runtime bundles to stop injecting the volatile Discord thread metadata, added session resolution by actual delivery target with normalized Discord identifiers, updated the agent command session resolver to look up delivery context (channel + to + accountId), and passed channel and replyAccount/accountId into the resolver; also fixed incorrect session key usage.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes were applied directly to compiled files in openclaw/dist/ (dist/pi-embedded-*.js, dist/gateway-cli-*.js, dist/compact-*.js, dist/register.agent-*.js) and will be lost on updates; maintainers should integrate fixes into source, rebuild distribution artifacts, and add a post-update re-application script or permanent release to avoid regression.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads repeatedly re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) each turn causing context degradation, and CLI routing (openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...) resolved to agent:*:main because the session resolver didn't use delivery context (channel+to+accountId); fixes: prevent metadata injection, normalize Discord targets, add delivery-context lookup in agent resolver, pass channel/replyAccount, and correct session key construction in compiled dist/*.js.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary of two related regressions: Discord thread metadata being re-injected each turn (listing exact fields) causing context bloat, and CLI agent commands resolving to the wrong session because the resolver ignored delivery context (channel+to+accountId), with targeted fixes noted.

results.title: [Bug]: tools/sandbox: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) is hardcoded and not user-configurable

summary.problem_summary: The sandbox media staging cap is hardcoded to 5 MB in dist/store-*.js (const MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024), so files larger than 5 MB are silently rejected or unavailable inside sandboxed agents. Issue present in v2026.3.7 and v2026.3.8.
summary.solution_summary: Read MEDIA_MAX_BYTES from configuration (e.g. openclaw.json under tools.media.maxBytes) instead of a hardcoded constant, aligning with per-type keys like tools.media.image.maxBytes and tools.media.audio.maxBytes; update code to use the configured limit and provide a sensible default.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Labels: bug, regression. Affects OpenClaw v2026.3.8 on Ubuntu 24 installed via npm. Reproducible by sending files >5 MB to sandbox.mode non-main. Current workaround: post-install sed patch to replace the constant in dist/store-*.js and restart the gateway.
summary.dedupe_summary: Sandbox media staging limit MEDIA_MAX_BYTES is hardcoded to 5*1024*1024 in dist/store-*.js, causing files over 5 MB to be rejected; request to make tools.media.maxBytes configurable via openclaw.json (issue seen in v2026.3.7 and v2026.3.8).

judge.reasoning: Concise summary captures the hardcoded MEDIA_MAX_BYTES, file location, and requested tools.media.maxBytes config change — the only minor noise is the unnecessary version numbers which aren't needed for deduping.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration being enabled

summary.problem_summary: Discord bot receives messages over the Gateway but all Discord REST API calls fail with 'fetch failed' despite proxy configured in openclaw.json and logs showing 'rest proxy enabled'; direct curl calls through the same proxy succeed, so the proxy itself works.
summary.solution_summary: No resolution provided in the issue; user suggests REST client is not using the configured proxy. Investigation needed into how the Discord REST client/fetch is handling per-provider proxy settings (no workaround or patch included).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reporter supplied config, logs and reproduction details (OpenClaw 2026.2.25 on macOS, Node 25.6.1); logs show 'rest proxy enabled' and gateway proxy enabled; no maintainer response or patch in the thread yet.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests return 'fetch failed' even though openclaw.json has 'proxy' and logs print 'rest proxy enabled'; Gateway WebSocket and Telegram (same proxy) work, and curl to Discord API via the same proxy succeeds — suggests Discord REST client in OpenClaw 2026.2.25 (Node 25.6.1, macOS) is not honoring proxy configuration.

judge.reasoning: Summary clearly states the core bug (Discord REST requests failing with 'fetch failed' despite proxy enabled and other clients working) but includes minor noisy details (specific OpenClaw/Node/macOS version) that aren't needed for deduplication.

results.title: Discord REST API (@buape/carbon) ignores channels.discord.proxy config

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls made by @buape/carbon's RequestClient ignore the channels.discord.proxy entry in openclaw.json — only the WebSocket gateway respects the proxy. The client calls globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware undici dispatcher, so REST requests (and memorySearch/attachment downloads) bypass the configured HTTP proxy on Node 22.
summary.solution_summary: Modify @buape/carbon's REST client to accept an undici dispatcher or explicit proxy configuration and have OpenClaw pass channels.discord.proxy to it so REST calls use the same proxy as the gateway. Short-term workaround: preload a proxy agent (NODE_OPTIONS=--require=proxy-preload.cjs) to call setGlobalDispatcher(new EnvHttpProxyAgent()), but avoid --use-env-proxy which breaks WebSocket upgrades.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Blocking for proxy-required deployments: gateway works but REST/embeddings/media downloads bypass proxy. A preload workaround exists; do not use NODE_OPTIONS=--use-env-proxy because it causes Discord WebSocket disconnections. Related: issue #25232 (attachment downloads bypass proxy).
summary.dedupe_summary: @buape/carbon RequestClient invokes globalThis.fetch without supplying an undici dispatcher, causing Discord REST API calls to ignore openclaw.json channels.discord.proxy; Node 22's native fetch doesn't honor HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY without --use-env-proxy (which in turn breaks WebSocket connections).

judge.reasoning: Concise, technically specific summary that identifies the root cause (RequestClient using globalThis.fetch without an undici dispatcher causing channels.discord.proxy to be ignored) and relevant Node 22 proxy behavior, so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: Performance degradation - context growth + lane blocking under load

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw shows performance degradation under sustained load with long-running conversations: unbounded context growth (writer ~42k tokens, reasoning ~90k tokens) increasing per-turn latency, Discord lane blocking causing serialization under load, and typing indicators stuck until TTL in some code paths.
summary.solution_summary: Applied configuration limits (acp.maxConcurrentSessions 12→6, agents.defaults.maxConcurrent 3→2, agents.defaults.subagents.maxConcurrent 8→4, agents.defaults.subagents.maxChildrenPerAgent 8→4, agents.defaults.contextTokens 80000) and increased channels.discord.eventQueue.listenerTimeout 120000→300000; improved patching script to cover hash variants; restarted gateway and observed no new "lane wait exceeded" or "typing TTL reached" errors, though restart is not a full fix under sustained load. Recommendation: make limits adaptive or expose them in openclaw config.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Maintainers lowered concurrency limits, imposed a 80k token context cap, extended Discord event queue listener timeout, and updated openclaw-rootfix-v2026.3.11.sh to patch all reply/compact/pi-embedded/gateway-cli/register.agent hash variants; gateway restart validated immediate symptom reduction but long-term mitigation requires config tuning.
summary.dedupe_summary: Unbounded context token growth (writer ~42k, reasoning ~90k) causing rising latency, Discord lane blocking and stuck typing; mitigated by reducing concurrency (acp.maxConcurrentSessions 12→6, agents.defaults.maxConcurrent 3→2, subagents 8→4), setting agents.defaults.contextTokens=80000, and increasing channels.discord.eventQueue.listenerTimeout 120000→300000; script hardening in openclaw-rootfix-v2026.3.11.sh.

judge.reasoning: Summary captures the core technical problem (unbounded context growth causing latency, lane blocking, stuck typing) and remedial configuration changes, but includes minor noisy details (exact config values and a script/version name) that aren't necessary for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread context re-injected every turn (context pollution + cache bust)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord threads repeatedly re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) on every conversation turn, causing context contamination, ballooning token usage, prompt-cache busting and performance degradation in long threads.
summary.solution_summary: A manual hotfix was applied to compiled dist files: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js prevents re-injection of the listed Discord thread fields, and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js adds an isNewSession guard for thread starter and replaces resolveAgentMainSessionKey with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...); validation shows thread context no longer repeats and prompt caching is restored.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High risk: changes were made directly to compiled dist/ files and will be lost on update/reinstall. Recommended actions: implement the fix in source, add the isNewSession guard in the session handler, correct session key construction, and add post-update revalidation to catch regressions; refer to related issues #41355, #34228, #37047.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) every turn, corrupting context and busting prompt cache; temporary fix applied in dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to stop injection and add an isNewSession guard, but the change must be ported to source to persist.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the root cause (Discord thread metadata re-injected each turn causing context pollution and cache bust) and the temporary dist-file fix, with no template noise, so it will cluster well with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread metadata re-injected every turn (context pollution)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord thread messages have volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) re-injected on every turn, causing context pollution, performance degradation in long threads, and prompt cache busting with higher token costs.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch was applied to the compiled file dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js to stop re-injecting those Discord thread metadata fields after the first turn; validation shows repeated context stops, prompt caching is effective again, and billed tokens drop significantly. The recommended next step is to implement a source-level fix and keep a post-update revalidation script.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk: the fix was made directly in compiled dist files and will be lost on update/reinstall. Maintainters should port the patch into source, release an official fix, and consider automated revalidation after updates. Related issues: #41355, #34228, #37047.
summary.dedupe_summary: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js re-injects Discord thread volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) on every turn, causing context pollution, token bloat and prompt cache invalidation; manual dist patch restricts injection to the first turn.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that names the affected compiled file, the exact metadata re-injected, the impact (context pollution/token bloat/cache invalidation), and the intended fix scope (restrict to first turn), so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads root fix - runtime patches for context growth + session routing

summary.problem_summary: Two runtime regressions in Discord thread handling: per-turn reinjection of metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, InboundHistory) causing unbounded context growth and performance/latency degradation, and the session resolver incorrectly falling back to agent:*:main instead of resolving live thread-bound sessions, causing CLI routing to the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: Applied direct runtime patches to compiled bundles (dist/reply-BCcP6j4h.js, dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js, dist/pi-embedded-Cz5VjpnY.js, dist/gateway-cli-B-E8XzUM.js, dist/register.agent-jS8B5meo.js) to stop per-turn metadata reinjection and make resolver lookups consistent by delivery context (channel+to+accountId); added idempotent re-application script and backups, restarted and validated the gateway.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Patches are manual edits to compiled bundles and will be lost on updates—maintainers must run the provided re-application script and keep backups (*.bak-rootfix-*); gateway restart and log checks passed and a verification checklist and recommended smoke/long-thread tests are documented.
summary.dedupe_summary: Runtime patches to compiled dist JS bundles to remove per-turn reinjection of message_id/reply_to_id/timestamp/Thread starter/InboundHistory that caused context growth in long Discord threads, and to fix session resolver lookup to use delivery context (channel+to+accountId) preventing fallback to agent:*:main; includes idempotent reapply script and backup files.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific about the two core fixes (removing per-turn metadata reinjection causing context growth and fixing session resolver to use delivery context), with only a minor operational detail about reapply scripts that isn't necessary for deduping.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord messages not being sent - "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw 2026.3.2 fails to send Discord reply messages with "TypeError: fetch failed" even though incoming messages are received and logs indicate both REST and gateway proxies are enabled. The configured HTTP proxy (http://127.0.0.1:7890) works for curl, but sending replies from the bot always fails in systemd-run Node 22 environment.
summary.solution_summary: No patch provided in the report; reporter suggests the Discord REST client is not applying the configured proxy when sending replies. Likely fix: ensure the fetch/http client used for Discord REST reply requests uses the configured proxy agent or proxy environment variables so outbound requests go through the Clash proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a regression (worked previously) and blocks outgoing replies while receives and model responses succeed; reproducible under systemd with Node 22 and OpenClaw 2026.3.2. Logs show 'discord: rest proxy enabled' and 'discord: gateway proxy enabled' yet final reply fails, indicating investigation into how fetch is invoked for reply sends is required.
summary.dedupe_summary: OpenClaw 2026.3.2: Discord replies fail with 'final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed' despite 'discord: rest proxy enabled' and an http://127.0.0.1:7890 Clash proxy configured; incoming messages and model responses work and curl via the proxy succeeds, implying Discord REST reply requests are not using the configured proxy (regression with Node 22, systemd).

judge.reasoning: Summary accurately states the core bug (Discord REST replies failing despite proxy config and incoming messages working) but includes minor noise (version/Node/systemd details) that aren't needed for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of live session

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the CLI command `openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...` routes messages to the generic session `agent:*:main` instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the compiled session resolver doesn't lookup by delivery context and Discord targets aren't normalized.
summary.solution_summary: A local manual patch was applied to dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js to normalize Discord targets (prefixing with 'channel:'), add session lookup by delivery context (channel + to + accountId), and only fallback to agent:*:main if no matching session is found; this makes CLI routing hit the correct live session.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk: change was made directly in compiled dist/ file and will be lost on update; fix needs to be implemented in source, covered by tests, and coordinated with related issues (#36401, #41562) to avoid reverting behavior.
summary.dedupe_summary: CLI routing bug: session resolver in dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js does not lookup by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) and fails to normalize Discord target IDs (e.g., '148...' vs 'channel:148...'), causing fallback to agent:*:main; patched by adding normalization and delivery-context session lookup.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the core bug (session resolver in dist/gateway-cli not using delivery-context lookup and failing to normalize Discord targets, causing fallback to agent:*:main) and would match similar routing/normalization issues.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Regression in compact - resolveAgentMainSessionKey ReferenceError

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 a regression in the compiled file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls a non-existent resolveAgentMainSessionKey(), causing ReferenceError at runtime and breaking the agent command and gateway processing.
summary.solution_summary: Manually patched dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey(...) with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...), restoring the session resolver and eliminating the ReferenceError; validation shows agent commands and new writer/reasoning sessions work correctly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a high-risk regression introduced by a prior fix (fix #3) and fixed only in compiled dist files; maintainers must apply the correction in source (not just dist) before reapplying or releasing to avoid the ReferenceError being reintroduced.
summary.dedupe_summary: dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls undefined resolveAgentMainSessionKey instead of the available buildAgentMainSessionKey, causing ReferenceError and agent command failures; manual hotfix replaces the call in the compiled bundle but will be lost on reinstall/upstream updates.

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that identifies the exact faulty compiled file, the incorrect function call causing a ReferenceError, and that a local hotfix exists but will be overwritten, which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord plugin fails with "fetch failed" when using proxy

summary.problem_summary: Discord plugin's REST API requests and health check ignore channels.discord.proxy (only WebSocket uses it), causing 'fetch failed' and message send timeouts in proxy-required environments despite curl via the proxy working.
summary.solution_summary: Patch the Discord channel to apply channels.discord.proxy to all HTTP/REST API calls and health checks (not just WebSocket), ensure the gateway passes the proxy to the HTTP/fetch client, add tests and release a fix.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression reported on OpenClaw 2026.3.8; labeled bug/regression. Reporter requests urgent fix so REST calls and health check respect channels.discord.proxy and message delivery works behind a proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests and health check bypass channels.discord.proxy (only WebSocket uses the proxy), causing 'fetch failed' and message send timeouts in proxy-required environments.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that focuses on the core bug — channels.discord.proxy not applied to REST API/health checks (only WebSocket), causing fetch failures — with no extraneous template text.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Session resolver in compact lacks delivery-context lookup

summary.problem_summary: En OpenClaw v2026.3.11 el session resolver del comando agent dentro de dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js no realiza lookup por delivery context (channel+to+accountId), por lo que resuelve sesiones a agent:*:main en vez de la sesión viva del canal/target; provoca inconsistencia entre routing del CLI gateway y el comando y respuestas mal asociadas a sesiones.
summary.solution_summary: Parche manual en dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js: se añadió lookup por delivery context (combinación channel + to + accountId) en el session resolver y se mantiene fallback a main solo si no se encuentra sesión específica; validado que el agent resuelve y asocia respuestas correctamente.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Parche aplicado directamente en archivos compilados en dist/ (high risk): será sobrescrito en actualizaciones/reinstalaciones; se requiere corrección en la fuente/build para persistir el arreglo y evitar regresiones en futuras releases.
summary.dedupe_summary: dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js (OpenClaw v2026.3.11) session resolver for agent command lacks delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId), causing fallback to agent:*:main and incorrect session association; manual compiled-file patch adds delivery-context lookup and fallback to main only if no specific session found.

judge.reasoning: Clearly and specifically states the core bug (compiled session resolver missing delivery-context lookup causing fallback to agent:*:main) and the resulting patch, giving strong, focused signal for deduplication.

results.title: [Feature Request] Add i18n support for OpenClaw Control UI (Chinese localization)

summary.problem_summary: The OpenClaw Control UI contains most interface text hardcoded in English (settings, buttons, agent/skill pages, channel configs, status messages), creating usability barriers for non-English users, particularly Chinese speakers.
summary.solution_summary: Extract hardcoded strings into an i18n layer (e.g., JSON locale files), add a zh-CN locale with translated strings, implement a language selector or auto-detect, and build reusable infrastructure to add more languages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Feature request marked Medium-High priority for UX accessibility; notes docs already have zh-CN translations and that the built UI bundle contains hardcoded strings; asks whether this is on the roadmap or open for contributors.
summary.dedupe_summary: Control UI hardcoded English strings; request to implement i18n starting with Simplified Chinese (zh-CN) by extracting strings to locale files, adding translations, and providing a language selector/auto-detect.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core feature request (implement i18n for Control UI, extract strings, add zh-CN translations, and language selector/auto-detect) with no boilerplate or extraneous details.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): add systemPrompt support for accounts and groups

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group chats couldn't apply channel-level systemPrompt settings at account or group granularity, preventing per-account and per-group prompt customization (including wildcard defaults) and causing unclear override semantics between root and account group maps.
summary.solution_summary: Adds systemPrompt support to WhatsApp config schema and resolution logic, supporting channels.whatsapp.systemPrompt, channels.whatsapp.accounts.<id>.systemPrompt, channels.whatsapp.groups.<groupId>.systemPrompt and account-level groups with wildcard '*'; implements account-over-root precedence, group-specific then wildcard fallback, account groups fully replace root groups, plus tests and docs.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Closes issue #7011 and replaces the author's prior larger PR; backward compatible with no security/network changes, limited scope to WhatsApp gateway/orchestration and integration code, includes tests/docs, and does not alter hot-reload or non-WhatsApp behavior (size M).
summary.dedupe_summary: Introduce WhatsApp systemPrompt hierarchy: channels.whatsapp.systemPrompt fallback, overridden by channels.whatsapp.accounts.<id>.systemPrompt; resolve group prompt from accounts.<id>.groups.<groupId> then '*' wildcard; account 'groups' map fully replaces root channels.whatsapp.groups (no merge); final injected prompt = account prompt + resolved group prompt joined with a blank line.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the new WhatsApp systemPrompt resolution hierarchy, wildcard/group semantics, replacement behavior, and final prompt composition—no boilerplate or irrelevant details.

results.title: Fix WhatsApp group append processing and account-scoped gatin

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group messages delivered via messages.upsert(type="append") were being dropped before agent processing, and group policy resolution omitted account-scoped data (no accountId), causing account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom and allowlist fallback to be ignored.
summary.solution_summary: Enable append processing only for WhatsApp group messages (DM append history still skipped) and propagate accountId when resolving WhatsApp group policy so account-scoped channels.whatsapp.accounts.<id> and its groupAllowFrom/allowFrom are considered for gating and allowlist fallback.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes touch src/web/inbound/monitor.ts and src/web/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.ts; no config/schema/dist changes, no debug logging added. Targeted tests and live traces verify groups now route and gate correctly; revert by restoring the two files if needed. Risks: processing backlog/history for groups may change behavior, but DM append remains skipped and tests cover account-scoped allowlist behavior.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp group append events were skipped before enqueueing and group policy helpers resolved without accountId, causing account-scoped channels.whatsapp.accounts.<id> groupAllowFrom/allowFrom fallback to be ignored; fix scopes append handling to groups and uses account-scoped account for allowlist fallback.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug and the fix: append handling for WhatsApp groups was skipped and policy resolution lacked account-scoping, now both are corrected to use group-scoped append processing and account-scoped allowlist fallback.

results.title: fix(gateway): resolve Canvas 401 behind Tailscale Serve

summary.problem_summary: Canvas (A2UI) returned 401 when accessed through Tailscale Serve because resolveClientIp() returned undefined for requests from a trusted proxy with no forwarding headers, and isLocalDirectRequest() did not fall back to the socket address when allowRealIpFallback was enabled and XFF resolved to a non-loopback Tailscale IP.
summary.solution_summary: Fixes return the socket address from resolveClientIp() when no forwarding headers are present, make isLocalDirectRequest() check req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is enabled, gate .ts.net host acceptance behind an existing auth.allowTailscale flag, thread allowTailscale through callers, and update docs and tests to reflect required gateway.trustedProxies and allowRealIpFallback settings.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Includes unit tests (auth/net), docs changes, and depends on PR #50636 (merge first); behavior is backward-compatible since allowTailscale defaults to true for tailscale.mode = "serve", and reviewers should inspect auth/net and server-http related files for the socket-fallback and .ts.net gating logic.
summary.dedupe_summary: Canvas returns 401 behind Tailscale Serve because resolveClientIp returned undefined for direct trusted-proxy requests without X-Forwarded headers and isLocalDirectRequest didn’t fallback to req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is true; fix adds socket-address fallback, gates .ts.net host-header acceptance behind auth.allowTailscale, and threads allowTailscale through callers.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the root causes (resolveClientIp and isLocalDirectRequest fallbacks), the concrete fixes (socket fallback, .ts.net gating, threading allowTailscale), and uses precise technical terms useful for deduplication.

results.title: fix(security): block non-self messages in WhatsApp self-chat mode

summary.problem_summary: checkInboundAccessControl() computed an isSelfChat heuristic but never used it as a guard, so when WhatsApp channel was configured without an explicit groupPolicy non-self messages (groups, DMs from strangers, broadcasts) could pass through because resolveWhatsAppRuntimeGroupPolicy() defaulted to "open". This violated self-chat mode's isolation intent by allowing non-self messages to reach downstream processing.
summary.solution_summary: Added an early-return guard in checkInboundAccessControl() that immediately rejects any non-self message when account.selfChatMode === true, placed before group/DM policy evaluation. The change intentionally checks only the explicit selfChatMode flag (not the heuristic isSelfChat) to avoid blocking teammates allowed via allowFrom; isSelfChat is still returned for UI/logging.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All existing access-control tests (9) pass; code-path analysis and targeted verifications were done and the guard placement is intentional. Change affects only WhatsApp inbound access-control (src/web/inbound/access-control.ts) and can be reverted by removing the early-return block; note that auto-detected self-chat (heuristic-only) remains unblocked by design.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp access-control bug: explicit selfChatMode=true did not block non-self messages because isSelfChat was computed but unused and groupPolicy defaulted to "open", allowing groups/strangers through; fix adds an early-return guard rejecting non-self messages when account.selfChatMode === true while preserving teammate allowFrom behavior.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that states the core WhatsApp self-chat access-control bug, root cause (unused isSelfChat + groupPolicy default), and the precise fix (early-return guard on account.selfChatMode) without boilerplate.

results.title: fix: store group messages from non-allowlisted senders as pending context

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy set to "allowlist", group messages from senders not in groupAllowFrom are dropped at access-control (allowed: false → continue) so the agent never sees them in the "Chat messages since your last reply" context block, breaking the documented flow where unmentioned messages should be stored for context.
summary.solution_summary: Adds an opt-in config flag groupContextFromAll (top-level and per-account, default false) that makes access-control return storeForContext for non-allowlisted senders, marks those inbound messages as contextOnly (no read receipts), and records them as pending group history so the agent sees them for context but they cannot trigger replies.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is opt-in and backwards-compatible (default false), small scope touching access-control, monitor, types, config schema and account resolution; labeled channel: whatsapp-web, size: S, stale. Greptile flagged a 3/5 confidence due to a potential allowlist normalization/matching edge case and the new path bypassing some group gating/state updates.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp allowlist behavior drops non-allowlisted group messages entirely; this PR introduces groupContextFromAll to store those messages as pending context (contextOnly) without triggering replies, threads a new access-control outcome (storeForContext) through inbound monitor, skips read receipts, and persists entries in group history (per-account or top-level config, default false).

judge.reasoning: Concise summary of the core bug (allowlist discards group messages) and the concrete fix (new groupContextFromAll config, storeForContext outcome, context-only storage, skip receipts, persist history) with specific terms that will match duplicates.

results.title: feat(sessions_spawn): add sessionKey param to reuse sub-agent sessions

summary.problem_summary: sessions_spawn always created a new random-UUID session for sub-agents, preventing deterministic session reuse and preserving conversation history across spawns; there was also a Greptile finding about a session key bypassing an allowlist.
summary.solution_summary: Adds an optional sessionKey parameter: short keys map to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys are accepted after validating the embedded agentId matches targetAgentId, and omission falls back to crypto.randomUUID(); includes four tests for deterministic key, UUID fallback, fully-qualified passthrough, and cross-agent mismatch rejection.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reviewer flagged three blocking issues: a test imports a non-existent module (openclaw-tools.js vs clawdbot-tools.js) causing compilation failure, a case-sensitivity bug in fully-qualified agentId validation (embedded ID not normalized), and inconsistent error handling (throw vs returning jsonResult), lowering confidence for merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: Introduce optional sessionKey to sessions_spawn to reuse sub-agent sessions: short keys namespace to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys validated to prevent cross-agent session injection, fallback to crypto.randomUUID(); tests added; problems found—broken test import, case-sensitive agentId mismatch bug, and inconsistent error handling.

judge.reasoning: The summary clearly describes the new optional sessionKey behavior, namespacing, validation, and fallback which are the core signals for deduplication, but it includes extra noise about broken tests and minor bugs that aren't needed for clustering.

results.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect captionable messages for editMessageCaption

summary.problem_summary: Editing Telegram messages that contain media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) used editMessageText and caused Telegram to return 400 "there is no text in the message to edit", so message(action=edit) on captionable messages failed and left the UI inconsistent.
summary.solution_summary: editMessageTelegram now detects the specific 400 error via NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE and retries with editMessageCaption for media messages; buttons-only updates call editMessageReplyMarkup directly; MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED is treated as success and HTML parse fallback preserved. Six unit tests were added covering retry, buttons-only path, error propagation, and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Backward-compatible change limited to src/telegram/send.ts, no public API changes; introduces calls to editMessageCaption and editMessageReplyMarkup and a regex to match Telegram's "no text" error; includes tests and can be reverted by restoring the previous send.ts. Risk: regex may fail if Telegram changes error wording—unmatched errors will continue to propagate as before.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram editMessageText fails for captionable media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) with 400 'there is no text in the message to edit'; fix adds NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE to catch that 400 and retry with editMessageCaption (and uses editMessageReplyMarkup for button-only edits), plus tests covering the retry and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling.

judge.reasoning: Clear, concise technical summary naming the exact failure (editMessageText 400 for captionable media) and the precise fix (catch specific 400, retry with editMessageCaption; use editMessageReplyMarkup for button-only edits), so it will cluster correctly with similar Telegram caption-edit issues.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): restore config-driven block streaming for WhatsApp delivery

summary.problem_summary: PR #24962 hardcoded disableBlockStreaming: true for WhatsApp to prevent reasoning leaks, which also inadvertently disabled block streaming entirely so messages were batched and sent only when the agent finished, ignoring per-account or global blockStreaming settings.
summary.solution_summary: Restore config-driven behavior: deliver callback now only suppresses tool payloads (not all non-final payloads) and disableBlockStreaming is derived from channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming falling back to agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault; upstream shouldSuppressReasoningPayload continues to filter reasoning content.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All inbound contract tests pass and live WhatsApp verification shows progressive delivery at text_end when configured; reasoning/tool payload suppression remains intact and defaults unchanged; reviewers should watch for any regression in shouldSuppressReasoningPayload.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block streaming was disabled by a hardcoded disableBlockStreaming:true in PR #24962; this PR makes block streaming respect per-account channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming and agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault and narrows suppression in the deliver callback to tool payloads while relying on shouldSuppressReasoningPayload upstream.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that captures the core change—removing the hardcoded disable, restoring per-account/global config-driven WhatsApp block streaming, and narrowing suppression to tool payloads while noting upstream reasoning filtering.

results.title: fix(telegram): fix gateway hang on video messages and increase media download limit

summary.problem_summary: Sending videos above the old 5MB media limit caused the Telegram gateway to hang indefinitely because undici's Agent.close() deadlocked when the HTTP response body was left unconsumed, making the entire Telegram channel unresponsive until restart.
summary.solution_summary: Raised default channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb from 5 to 20, fixed closeDispatcher to avoid Agent.close deadlock (fallback to destroy after a 3s timeout), added timeouts and retry logic to getFile and media downloads, made media download failures non-fatal so captions/text still forward, and extended UI metadata stripping; tests updated.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Default media limit changed (5MB→20MB) but remains configurable via channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb; key files touched: src/infra/net/ssrf.ts and src/telegram/bot.ts; monitor closeDispatcher 3s timeout logs and media download timeouts, and revert limit via config or revert commits if needed.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram gateway hung on video messages when unconsumed response bodies caused undici Agent.close deadlock; fix increases mediaMaxMb to 20, adds timeouts/retries to getFile/fetchRemoteMedia, makes download errors non-fatal, and implements a closeDispatcher fallback (destroy after 3s).

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that captures the core bug (undici Agent.close deadlock on unconsumed response bodies causing gateway hangs) and the key fixes (increase mediaMaxMb, timeouts/retries, non-fatal download errors, closeDispatcher fallback), with no boilerplate noise.

results.title: fix(telegram): use type-aware media placeholder for document attachments (#7116)

summary.problem_summary: Telegram inbound body fallback always used the hardcoded placeholder "<media:image>" for non-audio media when no caption/text was present, causing documents (PDF/DOCX) to be misidentified to the agent as images and leading to incorrect agent responses.
summary.solution_summary: Changed resolveTelegramInboundBody to use the already-computed, type-aware placeholder variable (e.g., <media:document>, <media:video>) instead of hardcoded <media:image>, and added unit tests in extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts and extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts to cover document and other media types; media download and extraction logic unchanged.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: PR closes #7116, scoped bugfix to agent-facing placeholder only (no changes to media download/processing), includes unit tests that validate placeholder text and message handling, and notes a small risk that downstream code pattern-matching on <media:image> could be affected.
summary.dedupe_summary: resolveTelegramInboundBody hardcoded "<media:image>" for non-audio inbound Telegram media, mislabeling documents as images; fix replaces the hardcoded string with the type-aware placeholder so document messages produce "<media:document>", plus unit tests to lock behavior.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary identifying the function, the mislabeling bug, and the fix (type-aware placeholder) with minimal noise, so it will cluster with similar Telegram media placeholder issues.

results.title: media: make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes

summary.problem_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) was hardcoded in src/media/store.ts so users could not override it, causing files larger than 5 MB to silently fail to stage into sandboxed agents and preventing processing of common large files (PDFs, presentations, high-res images).
summary.solution_summary: Adds new media.maxBytes config field (integer, optional, default 5 MB, max 500 MB) and replaces the hardcoded constant with getMediaMaxBytes()/setMediaMaxBytes(); the gateway reads the config at boot, error messages report the configured limit, and tests were updated and pass.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All 56 tests pass and bot review threads were addressed; reviewers should verify backward compatibility (deprecated exported constant retained), schema enforcement of the 500 MB cap, and that per-channel download limits remain untouched; beware disk-usage risk if users set very large limits.
summary.dedupe_summary: Make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes (default 5 MB, capped at 500 MB), replacing hardcoded 5MB in src/media/store.ts with getMediaMaxBytes()/setMediaMaxBytes(), applied at gateway boot, and update error messages to show the configured MB value; tests updated and passing.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific about the core change (making MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable, where it's applied, replacement of hardcoded constant, default and cap), but includes a minor testing note that is unnecessary for deduplication.

results.title: fix: pass file metadata to agent when Telegram media download fails

summary.problem_summary: When Telegram media download fails (due to exceeding mediaMaxMb default 5MB or Telegram Bot API getFile 20MB limit, or other API errors), the handler returned early and silently dropped the entire message so the agent received no ack, no visibility, and the user got no useful feedback.
summary.solution_summary: On download failure, extract file metadata (name, size, type, dimensions) from the Telegram message object and inject a metadata stub into msg.text describing the file and why it couldn't be downloaded (e.g. exceeds limit or download failed), then let the message continue through the normal pipeline so the agent can handle/respond.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a bug fix to the Telegram integration (src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts) and is backward-compatible; previous reply-to-user notifications were removed so the agent decides responses. Verified by code/path inspection (not live runtime), safe to revert the single commit if needed, and the change only mutates msg.text in error paths previously dropping messages.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram handler previously returned early on media download failures and dropped messages; now it builds a file metadata stub from the Telegram message object and injects it into msg.text so files exceeding mediaMaxMb (default 5MB) or Telegram getFile limits (20MB) and other download errors are forwarded to the agent with an explanatory warning.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that clearly states the bug (Telegram handler dropped messages on media download failure), the implemented fix (builds file metadata stub and injects it into msg.text), and the relevant conditions (mediaMaxMb and Telegram getFile limits), so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: feat(cli): add --session-key targeting for openclaw agent

summary.problem_summary: The openclaw agent CLI lacked a way to target stable session keys, forcing callers to rely on rotating sessionId UUIDs, sender-derived --to, or falling back to the agent main key, causing callback routing ambiguity for automation flows.
summary.solution_summary: Adds a new --session-key flag to openclaw agent, surfaces sessionKey through AgentCliOpts into resolveSessionKeyForRequest, maintains precedence (sessionKey > sessionId > --to > --agent), updates help/validation text, and adds tests for CLI parsing and gateway forwarding.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, low-risk change (label size: S) with tests passing and a stated confidence score of 5/5; changes touch register.agent.ts, agent-via-gateway.ts, AgentCliOpts, and corresponding tests; backward-compatible and opt-in.
summary.dedupe_summary: Introduce --session-key to openclaw agent CLI and forward sessionKey into resolveSessionKeyForRequest so callers can target stable session keys (e.g., agent:demo:slack:channel:c0123456789) instead of UUID sessionId; updates AgentCliOpts, register.agent.ts, agent-via-gateway.ts, help text, and adds unit tests.

judge.reasoning: Succinctly describes the new --session-key CLI flag, its purpose (target stable session keys vs UUID sessionId), and the key code/files changed, making it a strong signal for deduplication.

results.title: feat: separate group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization

summary.problem_summary: When channels.whatsapp is configured with groupPolicy: "allowlist" the current implementation uses groupAllowFrom (sender phone numbers) to gate all group interactions, preventing admins from allowlisting specific group JIDs, allowing anyone to chat in approved groups, or keeping slash commands restricted to owners while opening group chat to all participants.
summary.solution_summary: If groupPolicy is "allowlist" and a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists, inbound access control now gates by group JID membership in that mapping (only listed groups accepted) while leaving groupAllowFrom to authorize slash commands; if no groups mapping is present, the prior sender-based allowlist behavior is preserved.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Code changes touch src/web/inbound/access-control.ts (integrating resolveChannelGroupPolicy), update types JSDoc, and add a Vitest suite with four tests covering allowlist-by-group, blocking unapproved groups, legacy fallback, and command authorization separation; depends on PR #3326 and notes one stale inline comment to fix. Tests cover new branches and the change is described as localized and safe to merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp: change groupPolicy: "allowlist" behavior so that when channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists inbound group access is gated by group JID membership (allow any sender in approved groups), while groupAllowFrom remains the gate for slash commands; falls back to sender-based filtering when no groups mapping is present.

judge.reasoning: Accurately and concisely describes the core behavioral change (group JID-based gating when groups mapping exists, sender-based fallback, and command authorization via groupAllowFrom) using specific config terms relevant for clustering.

results.title: fix(telegram): propagate mediaMaxMb limit to outbound media reply delivery

summary.problem_summary: Configured Telegram mediaMaxMb is ignored when delivering final reply messages containing media; loadWebMedia() in the reply delivery path always falls back to the default 100 MB cap so lower account limits aren’t enforced.
summary.solution_summary: Threaded mediaMaxBytes through the Telegram outbound reply chain (bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts) and the native-commands path (bot-native-commands.ts) so loadWebMedia receives the configured maxBytes for replies; added unit tests to verify behavior and left direct-send logic unchanged.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Two new unit tests added and all related test suites pass (108 tests across delivery, dispatch, native-commands, bot-message); change is small, backward-compatible, author addressed review comments and marked the PR ready with minimal risk.
summary.dedupe_summary: Outbound Telegram replies ignored telegramAccounts[].mediaMaxMb (loadWebMedia fell back to 100 MB); this PR threads mediaMaxBytes through bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts and bot-native-commands.ts so replies enforce the configured media size limit, with unit tests verifying maxBytes is passed or omitted.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug and the code paths changed (including filenames and config key) that would match duplicates, but it includes a minor testing-detail noise about unit tests that isn't necessary for deduplication.

results.title: fix(telegram): add HTML parse fallback for media captions

summary.problem_summary: Sending media messages (photo, document, video, animation, audio, voice, video note) failed with a 400 "can't parse entities" error when captions contained invalid HTML entities because the media send path lacked the HTML-parse fallback that text messages use.
summary.solution_summary: Wrapped the media send callback in withTelegramHtmlParseFallback so on an HTML parse error the send is retried without parse_mode and with the raw caption; applied to all media types and covered by a new unit test that simulates a parse error then a successful retry.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Added a unit test 'retries media caption as plain text on HTML parse error'; all 52 tests pass. Change is backward-compatible, limited to send.ts media send callback, and can be reverted by restoring that function; risk is only visual differences in caption formatting when fallback occurs.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media send (sendPhoto/sendDocument/sendVideo/sendAnimation/sendAudio/sendVoice/video note) had no HTML-parse fallback, causing 400 parse-entity errors for captions with invalid HTML; this PR adds withTelegramHtmlParseFallback to retry without parse_mode and send the raw caption.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug (media captions lacked HTML-parse fallback causing 400 errors) and the fix (wrap sends with withTelegramHtmlParseFallback to retry without parse_mode), with no boilerplate or noise.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): make block streaming configurable and fix delivery race in block-reply pipeline

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp had block streaming effectively disabled (disableBlockStreaming hardcoded true), causing intermediate text blocks to be batched and sometimes resulting in a delivery race where neither blocks nor the final reply reached the user. A ternary bug also caused configured blockStreaming to be ignored (undefined vs false).
summary.solution_summary: Introduce channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming (per-account/global), add sensible coalescing defaults, and change the dispatcher to sendBlockReplyAsync which awaits confirmed delivery before marking sentContentKeys. Update process-message to read the account config and fix the ternary, update dispatch to await async block delivery, and add/update tests for both streaming paths.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Tests updated and new cases added; manual verification shows streaming off yields a single final reply and streaming on delivers intermediate blocks; PR targets root-cause fix of the silent-turn bug rather than the prior revert workaround. Labels: channel: whatsapp-web, channel: feishu, size: S.
summary.dedupe_summary: Block streaming was effectively disabled for WhatsApp via a hardcoded disableBlockStreaming=true and a ternary bug ignored configured blockStreaming, causing a race: optimistic enqueue of block replies marked sentContentKeys before actual delivery, suppressing final replies; fix adds channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming config, blockStreamingCoalesceDefaults, and sendBlockReplyAsync which awaits delivery before marking sentContentKeys.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the root cause (hardcoded disableBlockStreaming and ternary bug), the delivery race (sentContentKeys set before delivery), and the concrete fixes (config option, coalescing defaults, and awaiting delivery via sendBlockReplyAsync).

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked a group-level allowlist: only sender-level groupAllowFrom was supported, so specific Signal groups couldn't be explicitly allowed or authorized to run control commands independent of sender checks.
summary.solution_summary: Adds channels.signal.groups.<groupId> config plus SignalGroupConfig type and Zod schema, wires resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler to bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom for listed groups, and integrates group-level allow into command authorizers; docs updated with examples.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Addresses Greptile review by incorporating groupExplicitlyAllowed into commandGate authorizers, preserves hasGroupAllowFrom backwards-compat behavior, passes TypeScript checks and was manually tested; safe to merge with minimal risk.
summary.dedupe_summary: Introduce channels.signal.groups.<groupId> allowlist that bypasses sender-level groupAllowFrom and authorizes listed Signal groups for message delivery and control commands by wiring resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler and adding SignalGroupConfig and Zod schema.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core change—adds a Signal group-level allowlist that bypasses sender checks, integrates with the event handler for delivery/command authorization, and adds config/types/schema—no boilerplate or irrelevant details.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel had no group-level allowlisting via channels.signal.groups.<groupId>, so when groupPolicy was set to "allowlist" with group IDs in groupAllowFrom, group messages were silently dropped because the sender-level filter compared sender IDs to group IDs and always failed.
summary.solution_summary: Added a groups config to SignalAccountSchemaBase (requireMention, tools, toolsBySender) and updated Signal's event handler to call resolveChannelGroupPolicy for group-level checks before falling back to sender-level groupAllowFrom; added four tests and verified full test suite passes.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fixes #25540; small Signal-channel change with 4 new tests; all 624 config tests, 6 Signal monitor tests, and 17 group-policy tests pass. Labeled channel: signal, size: S. PR was AI-assisted and reviewed by @JamesPeck.
summary.dedupe_summary: Signal lacked group-level allowlisting (channels.signal.groups), so groupPolicy:"allowlist" with group IDs in groupAllowFrom failed because code compared sender phone/UUID to group IDs; fix adds groups schema and calls resolveChannelGroupPolicy in Signal access checks.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug and fix: missing group-level allowlist in Signal caused sender-vs-group ID mismatch and was resolved by adding groups schema and using resolveChannelGroupPolicy, which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): support config-driven block streaming

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp messages were being coalesced into a single final payload because process-message.ts had disableBlockStreaming hardcoded true, so intermediate agent blocks (tool calls/sleeps) were not delivered as separate WhatsApp messages.
summary.solution_summary: Make WhatsApp respect the channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming config by adding resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled(), replacing the hardcoded flag, registering blockStreaming capability and DEFAULT_BLOCK_STREAMING_COALESCE in dock.ts, and fixing delivery logic so intermediate blocks count as delivered. Tests updated to cover enabled/default/disabled behaviors.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is backwards-compatible by defaulting to the previous behavior when blockStreaming is omitted/false, aligns WhatsApp with Telegram/Line behavior, includes a small test update (process-message.inbound-contract.test.ts) and capability registration in dock.ts; labeled channel: whatsapp-web and size: S.
summary.dedupe_summary: process-message.ts hardcoded disableBlockStreaming:true causing WhatsApp to batch intermediate blocks; PR switches to config-driven channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming via resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled(), fixes queuedFinal-only delivery counting so intermediate payloads mark replies as delivered, registers blockStreaming capability and DEFAULT_BLOCK_STREAMING_COALESCE in dock.ts, and updates inbound contract tests.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core change (removing hardcoded disableBlockStreaming for WhatsApp, making it config-driven, fixing delivery-count logic, and registering capability) with relevant file names and constants, no boilerplate.
